title: Documentation
text: The pfas_birthweight package provides a suite of functions for loading, cleaning, merging, and analyzing PFAS drinking water contamination data alongside CDC birth weight outcomes. This page documents all available functions.

title: Documentation
text: The pfas_birthweight package provides a suite of functions for loading, cleaning, merging, and analyzing PFAS drinking water contamination data alongside CDC birth weight outcomes. This page documents all available functions.

title: Documentation
text:
Pipeline Functions

build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
The primary function that runs the entire pipeline end-to-end and returns a county-year panel ready for analysis.
Signature:
build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: A panel DataFrame with the following columns:




Column
Description




FIPS
5-digit county FIPS code


STATE
2-letter state abbreviation


COUNTY_SERVED
County name


year
Year (2016–2024)


births
Number of births


avg_birth_weight
Average birth weight (grams)


PFAS_county
Population-weighted mean PFAS concentration (ng/L)



Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
print(panel.head())



load_pfas()
Loads and cleans the raw PFAS sample data from the bundled Excel file, replacing non-detects with MRL/2.
Signature:
load_pfas()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Cleaned sample-level PFAS data with a result column in ng/L




collapse_pfas_to_pws()
Aggregates sample-level PFAS measurements to one row per public water system.
Signature:
collapse_pfas_to_pws(pfas_df)
Parameters:

pfas_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_pfas()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: One row per PWS ID with mean PFAS concentration and population served




load_geo()
Loads the EPA SDWA Geographic Areas file, keeping only county-level service area rows (AREA_TYPE_CODE == "CN").
Signature:
load_geo()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Filtered geographic areas with PWSID and COUNTY_SERVED columns




build_county_pfas()
Merges PWS-level PFAS data with the county mapping and computes population-weighted average PFAS per county.
Signature:
build_county_pfas(pws_pfas, geo_df)
Parameters:

pws_pfas (pd.DataFrame): Output of collapse_pfas_to_pws()
geo_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_geo()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: One row per (STATE, COUNTY_SERVED) with a PFAS_county column




load_crosswalk()
Loads the Census national county FIPS crosswalk and standardizes county names by removing suffixes like “County”, “City”, and “Parish”.
Signature:
load_crosswalk()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Crosswalk with STATE, FIPS, COUNTY_NAME, and COUNTY_CLEAN columns




attach_fips_to_pfas()
Joins 5-digit FIPS codes onto the county-level PFAS data using the Census crosswalk.
Signature:
attach_fips_to_pfas(county_pfas, crosswalk)
Parameters:

county_pfas (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_county_pfas()
crosswalk (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_crosswalk()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: county_pfas with a FIPS column attached




load_birth_weights()
Loads the CDC WONDER birth weight data, dropping suppressed cells and CDC footer rows.
Signature:
load_birth_weights()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Panel data with columns FIPS, year, births, and avg_birth_weight




build_panel()
Merges the birth weight panel with county-level PFAS exposure data on FIPS code.
Signature:
build_panel(bw_df, pfas_fips)
Parameters:

bw_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_birth_weights()
pfas_fips (pd.DataFrame): Output of attach_fips_to_pfas()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Final panel sorted by FIPS and year




load_birthweight_counts()
Loads CDC WONDER birth weight category counts and computes a low birth weight (LBW) rate per county-year. LBW is defined as births under 2,500 grams.
Signature:
load_birthweight_counts()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Panel data with columns FIPS, year, lbw_births, total_births, and lbw_rate




load_gestational_age()
Loads CDC WONDER gestational age category counts and computes a preterm birth rate per county-year. Preterm is defined as births under 37 weeks gestation.
Signature:
load_gestational_age()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Panel data with columns FIPS, year, preterm_births, total_births, and preterm_rate




build_extended_panel()
Extends the base panel with low birth weight rates and preterm rates by merging in outputs from load_birthweight_counts() and load_gestational_age().
Signature:
build_extended_panel()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Panel DataFrame with all columns from build_pfas_birthweight_panel() plus lbw_births, total_births, lbw_rate, preterm_births, and preterm_rate

Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_extended_panel

panel = build_extended_panel()
print(panel.head())



load_ucmr5()
Loads and cleans the UCMR5 monitoring data, converting results from µg/L to ng/L and replacing non-detects with MRL/2. MRLs are joined from a separate lookup file.
Signature:
load_ucmr5()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Sample-level UCMR5 data with a result column in ng/L and a MRL column




collapse_ucmr5_to_pws()
Aggregates sample-level UCMR5 measurements to one row per public water system.
Signature:
collapse_ucmr5_to_pws(ucmr5_df)
Parameters:

ucmr5_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_ucmr5()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: One row per PWS ID with mean PFAS concentration




load_ucmr5_population()
Extracts population served per PWS from the existing PFAS.xlsx file, for use in population-weighting UCMR5 results.
Signature:
load_ucmr5_population()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: One row per PWS ID with a population_served column




attach_population_to_ucmr5()
Joins population served onto the PWS-level UCMR5 data. Systems with no population match are assigned a weight of 1.
Signature:
attach_population_to_ucmr5(pws_ucmr5, pop_df)
Parameters:

pws_ucmr5 (pd.DataFrame): Output of collapse_ucmr5_to_pws()
pop_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_ucmr5_population()

Returns:

pd.DataFrame: pws_ucmr5 with a population_served column attached




build_ucmr5_panel()
Builds a county-year panel using UCMR5 PFAS data instead of the primary PFAS source, filtered to 2023–2024 to match UCMR5 coverage. Includes all three birth outcomes.
Signature:
build_ucmr5_panel()
Returns:

pd.DataFrame: Panel DataFrame with the same column structure as build_extended_panel(), restricted to 2023–2024

title: Documentation
text:
Analysis Functions

plot_eda()
Produces two side-by-side exploratory plots of the panel data.
Signature:
plot_eda(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_pfas_birthweight_panel()

Plots produced:

Scatter plot of PFAS concentration vs average birth weight with an OLS trend line
Average birth weight over time (2016–2024) by state

Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel, plot_eda

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
plot_eda(panel)



run_regression()
Runs an OLS regression of average birth weight on PFAS concentration with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county.
Signature:
run_regression(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_pfas_birthweight_panel()

Returns:

RegressionResultsWrapper: A fitted statsmodels results object

Model:
\[\text{BirthWeight}_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Where \(\gamma_t\) represents year fixed effects. County fixed effects are excluded by design since PFAS exposure is time-invariant and would be fully absorbed.
Example:
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel, run_regression

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
model = run_regression(panel)



plot_extended_eda()
Produces a 2×3 grid of exploratory plots covering all three birth outcomes against PFAS concentration and over time.
Signature:
plot_extended_eda(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_extended_panel()

Plots produced:

Row 1: Scatter plots of PFAS concentration vs average birth weight, LBW rate, and preterm rate, each with an OLS trend line
Row 2: Time trends for each outcome by state (2016–2024)

Output: Saves eda_extended_plots.png to the working directory



run_extended_regression()
Runs OLS regressions of all three birth outcomes on PFAS concentration with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county.
Signature:
run_extended_regression(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_extended_panel()

Returns:

dict: A dictionary keyed by outcome column name (avg_birth_weight, lbw_rate, preterm_rate), each containing a fitted statsmodels results object

Model:
\[Y_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Where \(Y_{ct}\) is the birth outcome in county \(c\) and year \(t\), and \(\gamma_t\) represents year fixed effects.



plot_ucmr5_nondetect_table()
Renders a formatted table summarizing UCMR5 PFAS monitoring results, showing MRLs, sample counts, and detection rates for each compound.
Signature:
plot_ucmr5_nondetect_table(ucmr5_df)
Parameters:

ucmr5_df (pd.DataFrame): Output of load_ucmr5()

Output: Saves pfas_nondetect_table.png to the working directory



plot_pfas_choropleth()
Renders an interactive Plotly choropleth map of county-level PFAS concentrations for the most recent year in the panel.
Signature:
plot_pfas_choropleth(panel)
Parameters:

panel (pd.DataFrame): Output of build_extended_panel()

Output: Saves pfas_choropleth.png to the working directory. Requires geopandas and plotly with image export support.



plot_regression_table()
Renders a formatted summary table of OLS regression results across all three birth outcomes.
Signature:
plot_regression_table(models)
Parameters:

models (dict): Output of run_extended_regression()

Table columns: Outcome, PFAS coefficient, standard error, 95% confidence interval, p-value, R², and observation count. Significance stars follow the convention *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1.
Output: Saves regression_table.png to the working directory

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
This project investigates the relationship between PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination in public drinking water systems and birth outcomes at the county level. Using data from the EPA, Census Bureau, and CDC, we construct a county-year panel dataset covering six states (Alabama, Arkansas, Missouri, New Jersey, Ohio, and Virginia) from 2016 to 2024.
Explore the data interactively with our Streamlit app here!
View our GitHub Repo here!

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
This project investigates the relationship between PFAS (per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances) contamination in public drinking water systems and birth outcomes at the county level. Using data from the EPA, Census Bureau, and CDC, we construct a county-year panel dataset covering six states (Alabama, Arkansas, Missouri, New Jersey, Ohio, and Virginia) from 2016 to 2024.
Explore the data interactively with our Streamlit app here!
View our GitHub Repo here!

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
Data Sources

EPA UCMR PFAS Monitoring Data (PFAS.xlsx): Sample-level PFAS measurements (ng/L) from public water systems across the six study states
EPA UCMR5 Data (UCMR5.xlsx): A second round of PFAS monitoring data (2023–2025) converted from µg/L to ng/L to match the original pipeline; contaminant-level minimum reporting levels (MRLs) are joined from a companion lookup file (UCMR_min_reporting_level.xlsx)
EPA SDWA Geographic Areas (SDWA_GEOGRAPHIC_AREAS.csv): Maps public water system IDs to the counties they serve
Census National County FIPS Crosswalk (national_county.txt): Links county names to standardized 5-digit FIPS codes
CDC WONDER Birth Weight Data (birth_weights.csv): County-level average birth weight and total births by year (2016–2024)
CDC WONDER Birth Weight Counts (Birthweight_Counts.xls): County-year birth counts broken out by birth weight category, used to compute low birth weight (LBW) rates
CDC WONDER Gestational Age Data (Gestational_age.xls): County-year birth counts broken out by gestational age category, used to compute preterm birth rates

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
Pipeline
The pipeline is packaged as pfas-birthweight, available on PyPI. It can be installed and run with:
pip install pfas-birthweight
The package exposes three panel-building entry points:







Function
Description




build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
Original panel: average birth weight + PFAS (UCMR 3/4), 2016–2024


build_extended_panel()
Adds LBW rate and preterm rate outcomes to the original panel


build_ucmr5_panel()
UCMR5-based PFAS estimates merged with 2023–2024 outcomes (avg birth weight, LBW rate, preterm rate)




Pipeline Steps (shared across all three builders)

Load and clean PFAS data: Raw sample-level measurements are loaded and non-detects are replaced with half the minimum reporting level (MRL/2), a standard method in environmental health literature. For UCMR5 data, measurements are converted from µg/L to ng/L and MRLs are joined from a separate lookup table.
Collapse to PWS level: Samples are averaged to the public water system (PWS) level. Population served is retained from PFAS.xlsx for both the original and UCMR5 paths.
Merge with EPA geographic areas: PWS IDs are matched to counties using the EPA SDWA geographic areas dataset, filtering for county-level service area codes (AREA_TYPE_CODE == "CN").
Aggregate to county level: PFAS concentrations are aggregated to the county level using population-weighted averages, grouped by state and county to avoid collapsing same-named counties across states.
Attach FIPS codes: County names are standardized (stripping “County”, “City”, “Parish” suffixes) and matched to 5-digit FIPS codes using the Census crosswalk.
Merge with birth weight data: The county-level PFAS estimates are merged onto the CDC WONDER panel using FIPS codes, producing a county-year dataset ready for analysis.
Attach LBW and preterm rates (extended and UCMR5 panels only): Low birth weight births (< 2,500 g, using four weight categories) and preterm births (< 37 weeks, using five gestational age categories) are summed per county-year and divided by total births to produce rate variables.

title: PFAS & Birth Weight Analysis
text:
Analysis
Three outcomes are analyzed:

Average birth weight (grams) — the primary outcome
Low birth weight (LBW) rate — share of births under 2,500 g
Preterm rate — share of births under 37 weeks gestation

All three are regressed on county-level PFAS concentration using OLS with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county:
\[\text{Outcome}_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Where \(\gamma_t\) represents year fixed effects controlling for national trends over time. Because PFAS exposure is measured as a single cross-sectional value per county, county fixed effects are excluded by design — they would absorb the exposure variable entirely.
Regression results across all three outcomes are summarized in a formatted table via plot_regression_table().

UCMR5 Supplement
A separate analysis uses UCMR5 monitoring data (2023–2025) merged with 2023–2024 birth outcome data. Because all UCMR5 PFAS compounds in the study states fell below their minimum reporting levels, a non-detect summary table is produced via plot_ucmr5_nondetect_table() in place of regression results.

title: Technical Report
text: This report documents the workflow used to collect, clean, merge, and analyze PFAS drinking water contamination data alongside CDC birth weight and gestational age records. It is intended to make the analysis reproducible by showing the full path from raw EPA monitoring data to the final county-year panel and downstream regression results.

title: Technical Report
text: This report documents the workflow used to collect, clean, merge, and analyze PFAS drinking water contamination data alongside CDC birth weight and gestational age records. It is intended to make the analysis reproducible by showing the full path from raw EPA monitoring data to the final county-year panel and downstream regression results.

title: Technical Report
text:
1. Data Sources
The analysis draws on four primary data sources:

EPA UCMR PFAS Monitoring Data: Sample-level PFAS measurements (ng/L) from public water systems, including minimum reporting levels and population served
EPA SDWA Geographic Areas: Maps public water system IDs to the counties they serve
Census National County FIPS Crosswalk: Links county names to standardized 5-digit FIPS codes
CDC WONDER Weight at Birth: County-year panel data on average birth weight, birth counts, low birth weight rates, and preterm rates from 2016–2024
CDC WONDER Gestational Age at Birth: County-year panel data on average gestational age and number of births at different gestational ages

title: Technical Report
text:
2. Pipeline
The pipeline is implemented in the pfas_birthweight package, available on PyPI. The main entry point is build_extended_panel(), which runs the full pipeline end-to-end and returns a county-year DataFrame ready for analysis.
The pipeline runs through the following steps:

Load and clean PFAS data: Raw sample-level measurements are loaded from the EPA monitoring file. Non-detects are replaced with half the minimum reporting level (MRL/2), a standard imputation method in environmental health research.
Collapse to PWS level: Samples are averaged to an unweighted mean at the public water system level, retaining population served for later weighting.
Merge with EPA geographic areas: PWS IDs are matched to counties using the EPA SDWA geographic areas dataset, filtering for county-level service area codes.
Aggregate to county level: PFAS concentrations are aggregated to the county level using population-weighted averages, grouping by state and county to avoid collapsing same-named counties across states.
Attach FIPS codes: County names are standardized and matched to 5-digit FIPS codes using the Census crosswalk, removing suffixes such as “County”, “Parish”, and “City”.
Merge with birth outcome data: County-level PFAS estimates are merged onto the CDC WONDER panel by FIPS code, producing a county-year dataset. Low birth weight rates and preterm rates are joined from separate CDC WONDER extracts.
Merge with gestational age data: County-level gestational age estimates are merged onto the CDC WONDER panel by FIPS code, producing a comprehensive county-year dataset.

Data limitations: Two sources of attrition merit note. CDC WONDER suppresses cells for counties with fewer than 10 births in a given year for any category of birth. For example, if there were fewer than 10 births under 3000g in a given year, that cell would be suppressed, which may undersample rural counties disproportionately. Additionally, not all PWS IDs match to a county via the EPA geographic areas file, and not all county names match cleanly to the Census crosswalk — unmatched counties are excluded from the analysis. Non-detect imputation as MRL/2 follows standard practice.

title: Technical Report
text:
3. Data Structure
The final panel produced by build_extended_panel() contains the following columns:







Column
Description




FIPS
5-digit county FIPS code


STATE
2-letter state abbreviation


COUNTY_SERVED
County name


year
Year (2016–2024)


births
Number of births


avg_birth_weight
Average birth weight (grams)


PFAS_county
Population-weighted mean PFAS concentration (ng/L)


lbw_rate
Low birth weight rate (births under 2,500g / total births)


preterm_rate
Preterm rate (births under 37 weeks / total births)



This structure separates what was sourced directly from the raw data files from what was derived during the pipeline, making it straightforward to trace any column back to its origin.

title: Technical Report
text:
4. Analysis
The final dataset contains 404 unique counties across 6 states with 747 county-year observations. Analysis is performed using OLS regression with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county:
\[Y_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Where \(Y_{ct}\) is the birth outcome in county \(c\) and year \(t\), and \(\gamma_t\) represents year fixed effects controlling for national trends over time. Because PFAS exposure is collapsed to a single cross-sectional value per county, county fixed effects are excluded by design as they would absorb the exposure variable entirely. Standard errors are clustered at the county level to account for serial correlation within counties over time. Based on prior environmental health literature, we expect \(\beta < 0\) for birth weight and \(\beta > 0\) for low birth weight and preterm rates.
Three outcomes are modeled separately: average birth weight, low birth weight rate, and preterm rate. Results are presented alongside 95% confidence intervals and significance stars following the convention *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1.

title: Technical Report
text:
5. Exploratory Data Analysis
Prior to regression, we examine the raw relationships between PFAS concentration and each birth outcome across the 404 counties in the sample. The scatter plots below show cross-sectional associations for the most recent year, while the time trend charts track state-level averages from 2016–2024.



PFAS & Birth Outcomes — Extended Exploratory Analysis


The scatter plots suggest a modest negative association between PFAS concentration and average birth weight, consistent with prior literature. The relationship with low birth weight rate is weakly positive, and the preterm rate trend line is nearly flat, suggesting limited cross-sectional signal in the raw data.The time trends reveal meaningful variation across states. Alabama consistently shows the lowest average birth weights and highest preterm rates, while New Jersey and Virginia tend to perform better on both measures. Year fixed effects are included in the regression to account for the fact that all states trend downward in birth weight over time. We aimed to absorb that shared national pattern so the PFAS coefficient reflects cross-county differences in exposure rather than a common time trend.

title: Technical Report
text:
6. Regression Results
OLS regression results are presented for all three birth outcomes in the table below. Across all three models, the coefficient on PFAS concentration is small in magnitude and statistically indistinguishable from zero. Average birth weight shows the largest point estimate at -9.18 grams per ng/L increase in PFAS (p=0.194), but the confidence interval [-23.02, 4.67] comfortably spans zero. The LBW rate coefficient is 0.0012 (p=0.571) and the preterm rate coefficient is -0.0016 (p=0.427), both negligible in magnitude. R² values across all three models are below 0.04, indicating that PFAS concentration and year fixed effects together explain very little of the variation in birth outcomes in this sample.



OLS Regression Results


These null results should be interpreted carefully rather than taken as evidence that PFAS exposure has no effect on birth outcomes. Several features of the data and research design likely limit statistical power:
Limited EPA reporting coverage. PFAS concentrations in the sample range from approximately 3.5 to 8.5 ng/L, but this likely understates true exposure. The EPA’s monitoring programs test for a limited subset of PFAS compounds, leaving many variants undetected. With most counties clustered in a narrow band of reported concentrations, the exposure variable may not capture meaningful differences in total PFAS burden across counties.
Ecological aggregation. County-level averages are a coarse proxy for individual exposure. Measurement error introduced by aggregating across all water systems within a county could attenuate the PFAS coefficient toward zero. Studies that find significant associations typically use individual-level biomarker data rather than area-level estimates.
Geographic scope. The sample covers six states and 404 counties, which is not nationally representative. If the relationship between PFAS and birth outcomes varies by region for reasons such as differences in baseline health, economic demographics, or co-exposures then estimates from this sample may not generalize.
Time-invariant exposure. Because PFAS is collapsed to a single county-level average across the full monitoring period, the regression cannot exploit within-county changes in exposure over time. Any causal identification relies entirely on cross-sectional variation, which is more susceptible to confounding by unobserved county characteristics.
Taken together, these limitations suggest the null result is likely a product of data constrains and should not be taken as conclusive. Future work with broader geographic coverage, individual-level exposure data, or longitudinal PFAS measurements would provide a stronger test of the relationship.

title: Technical Report
text:
7. Reproducibility
To reproduce the analysis:

Install the package: pip install pfas-birthweight
Ensure the bundled data files are present (included in the PyPI distribution)
Run the pipeline:

from pfas_birthweight import build_extended_panel

panel = build_extended_panel()
The pipeline is deterministic given the same input files. If the underlying EPA or CDC data files are updated, the package version should be incremented and the analysis rerun from scratch.

title: Technical Report
text:
8. Exploration
After building the panel, the dataset can be explored interactively using the Streamlit app:
streamlit run app.py
The app provides scatter plots of PFAS concentration against each birth outcome, county-level choropleth maps toggling between PFAS and outcome variables, time trend charts by state, and a regression results table. Each of these visualizations is filterable by state and year.

title: Getting Started
text:
Install the package directly from PyPI:
pip install pfas-birthweight
The package requires Python 3.9+ and will automatically install the following dependencies:

pandas — data manipulation
numpy — numerical operations
openpyxl — Excel file reading
statsmodels — regression analysis
matplotlib — plotting

title: Getting Started
text:
Install the package directly from PyPI:
pip install pfas-birthweight
The package requires Python 3.9+ and will automatically install the following dependencies:

pandas — data manipulation
numpy — numerical operations
openpyxl — Excel file reading
statsmodels — regression analysis
matplotlib — plotting

title: Getting Started
text:
Quick Start

1. Build the Panel
All data is bundled with the package. Three panel builders are available depending on your analysis goals:
Original panel (average birth weight, 2016–2024):
from pfas_birthweight import build_pfas_birthweight_panel

panel = build_pfas_birthweight_panel()
print(panel.head())
Extended panel (adds low birth weight rate and preterm rate):
from pfas_birthweight import build_extended_panel

panel = build_extended_panel()
print(panel.head())
UCMR5 panel (uses 2023–2025 PFAS monitoring data, filtered to 2023–2024 outcomes):
from pfas_birthweight import build_ucmr5_panel

panel = build_ucmr5_panel()
print(panel.head())
Each builder runs through the same core steps:

Load and clean raw PFAS sample data, replacing non-detects with MRL/2
Collapse measurements to the public water system (PWS) level
Merge with EPA geographic areas to map PWS IDs to counties
Aggregate to population-weighted county-level PFAS estimates
Attach 5-digit FIPS codes using the Census crosswalk
Merge with CDC WONDER birth weight data by FIPS and year

The extended and UCMR5 builders additionally compute low birth weight (LBW) rates and preterm birth rates from CDC WONDER birth count and gestational age files.


2. Explore the Data
# Check coverage
print(f"Counties: {panel['FIPS'].nunique()}")
print(f"States:   {panel['STATE'].unique()}")
print(f"Years:    {sorted(panel['year'].unique())}")

# View a specific state
print(panel[panel['STATE'] == 'OH'].head())
The resulting panel contains the following columns:







Column
Description




FIPS
5-digit county FIPS code


STATE
2-letter state abbreviation


COUNTY_SERVED
County name


year
Year (2016–2024)


births
Number of births


avg_birth_weight
Average birth weight (grams)


PFAS_county
Population-weighted mean PFAS concentration (ng/L)


lbw_births
Number of low birth weight births (extended/UCMR5 only)


total_births
Total births from weight-category file (extended/UCMR5 only)


lbw_rate
Share of births under 2,500 g (extended/UCMR5 only)


preterm_births
Number of preterm births (extended/UCMR5 only)


preterm_rate
Share of births under 37 weeks gestation (extended/UCMR5 only)





3. Plot the Data
Original EDA (PFAS vs average birth weight + time trends by state):
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import plot_eda

plot_eda(panel)
This produces two side-by-side plots saved to eda_plots.png:

A scatter plot of PFAS concentration vs average birth weight with a trend line
Average birth weight over time (2016–2024) by state

Extended EDA (all three outcomes):
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import plot_extended_eda

plot_extended_eda(panel)
This produces a 2×3 grid saved to eda_extended_plots.png:

Row 1: Scatter plots of PFAS vs average birth weight, LBW rate, and preterm rate (each with trend line)
Row 2: Time trends for each outcome by state

UCMR5 non-detect table (when all compounds fall below MRL):
from pfas_birthweight.pipeline import load_ucmr5
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import plot_ucmr5_nondetect_table

ucmr5_df = load_ucmr5()
plot_ucmr5_nondetect_table(ucmr5_df)
Produces a formatted table (pfas_nondetect_table.png) summarizing each PFAS compound, its MRL (µg/L), total samples tested, detections, and detection rate.
Choropleth map of county-level PFAS concentrations (requires geopandas and plotly):
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import plot_pfas_choropleth

plot_pfas_choropleth(panel)


4. Run the Regressions
Original regression (average birth weight only):
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import run_regression

model = run_regression(panel)
Runs OLS of average birth weight on PFAS concentration with year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by county:
\[\text{BirthWeight}_{ct} = \beta \cdot \text{PFAS}_{c} + \gamma_t + \varepsilon_{ct}\]
Extended regressions (all three outcomes):
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import run_extended_regression, plot_regression_table

models = run_extended_regression(panel)
plot_regression_table(models)
run_extended_regression() fits the same OLS specification for average birth weight, LBW rate, and preterm rate, printing a summary for each. plot_regression_table() renders a formatted comparison table (regression_table.png) showing the PFAS coefficient, standard error, 95% confidence interval, p-value, R², and observation count for each outcome. Significance stars follow the convention: *** p<0.01, ** p<0.05, * p<0.1.

title: Getting Started
text:
Saving the Panel
If you plan to run analysis scripts repeatedly, save the panel to CSV to avoid rebuilding the pipeline each time:
panel.to_csv("panel.csv", index=False)
Then load it directly in subsequent scripts:
import pandas as pd
panel = pd.read_csv("panel.csv", dtype={"FIPS": str})

title: Getting Started
text:
Full Example Script
from pfas_birthweight import build_extended_panel
from pfas_birthweight.analysis import (
    plot_eda,
    plot_extended_eda,
    run_regression,
    run_extended_regression,
    plot_regression_table,
)

# Build panel
panel = build_extended_panel()
print(f"Panel shape: {panel.shape}")

# EDA
plot_eda(panel)
plot_extended_eda(panel)

# Regressions
run_regression(panel)
models = run_extended_regression(panel)
plot_regression_table(models)
